q: What is the definition of a mutation according to the laboratory text?
o: A normal variation in cell growth
A change in the DNA at a particular locus in an organism
The physical exchange of chromosome segments during meiosis
A reversible alteration in RNA translation
e: A mutation is defined as a change in the DNA at a particular Locus in an organism, including point mutations and chromosomal changes.

q: Mutations that occur in gametes are classified as:
e: Germ line mutations are mutations occurring in gametes.

q: Somatic mutations are defined as mutations occurring in:
o: Any cell in the body except germ cells
Only the X and Y chromosomes
e: Somatic mutations are mutations occurring in any cell in the body except germ cells.

q: Autosomal mutations occur within genes located on:
e: Autosomal mutations are mutations within genes located on autosomes (chromosomes 1-22).

q: Mutations within genes located on the X-chromosome are called:
e: X-Linked mutations are mutations within genes located on the X-chromosome.

q: Which of the following best describes a Point Mutation?
o: A change involving the entire chromosome set
The addition, deletion, inversion, or substitution of a few bases
The loss of a large chromosome segment
The failure of homologous chromosomes to separate
e: Point Mutation involves the addition, deletion, inversion, or substitution of a few bases within genes.

q: A Transition mutation at the DNA level occurs when:
o: A purine is replaced by a pyrimidine
A pyrimidine is replaced by a purine
A purine is replaced by a different purine
A base is entirely deleted from the sequence
e: Transition: Purine replaced by different Purine, or Pyrimidine replaced by different Pyrimidine.

q: Which of the following substitutions is an example of a Transition?
e: Transition examples include AT -> GC and GC -> AT.

q: A Transversion mutation occurs when:
o: A purine is replaced by a different purine
A purine is replaced by a pyrimidine
A pyrimidine is replaced by a different pyrimidine
e: Transversion: Purine replaced by Pyrimidine, or Pyrimidine replaced by a Purine.

q: Which of the following is an example of a Transversion?
e: Transversion examples include AT -> CG and GC -> CG.

q: If a Pyrimidine is replaced by a different Pyrimidine, the mutation is termed:
e: Transition: Pyrimidine replaced by different Pyrimidine.

q: If a Pyrimidine is replaced by a Purine, the mutation is termed:
e: Transversion: Pyrimidine replaced by a Purine.

q: A Frame shift mutation involves:
o: Substituting one base for another without changing the reading frame
Inserting or deleting one or more base pairs that alters the reading frame
Reversing the sequence of a chromosome segment
A change in the ploidy level of the cell
e: Frame shift mutations involve inserting or deleting one or more base pairs, which alters the reading frame.

q: The addition of one or more extra nucleotides into the DNA is called:
e: Insertion: addition of one or more extra nucleotides into the DNA.

q: The removal of one or more nucleotides from the DNA is called:
e: Deletion: removal of one or more nucleotides from the DNA.

q: Chromosomal mutations are classified into which two main categories?
e: Chromosomal mutations are classified into Numerical mutations and Structural mutations.

q: Numerical chromosomal mutations involve a change in the:
o: Sequence of genes on a chromosome
e: Numerical mutations: change in number of chromosomes.

q: Euploidy is defined as a condition where the chromosome number is:
o: An exact multiple of the haploid chromosome number (n)
Not a multiple of the haploid number
e: Euploidy is an exact multiple of the haploid chromosome number (n).

q: Aneuploidy is defined as a condition where the chromosome number is:
o: An exact multiple of the haploid chromosome number (n)
Abnormal and not a multiple of the haploid number (n)
e: Aneuploidy: abnormal number of chromosomes, not a multiple of n.

q: The loss of both pairs of homologous chromosomes is denoted by the formula:
e: Nullisomy: loss of both pairs of homologous chromosomes (2n - 2).

q: Nullisomy represents the:
o: Loss of both pairs of homologous chromosomes
Gain of an extra pair of homologous chromosomes
e: Nullisomy: loss of both pairs of homologous chromosomes.

q: Monosomy is characterized by the:
o: Loss of both pairs of homologous chromosomes
Gain of an extra pair of homologous chromosomes
e: Monosomy: loss of a single chromosome (2n - 1).

q: Trisomy is represented by which formula?
e: Trisomy: gain of a single chromosome (2n + 1).

q: Tetrasomy is characterized by the:
o: Gain of an extra pair of homologous chromosomes
Loss of both pairs of homologous chromosomes
e: Tetrasomy: gain of an extra pair of homologous chromosomes (2n + 2).

q: Down Syndrome is an example of an autosomal aneuploid characterized by:
e: Down Syndrome is Trisomy - 21.

q: Patau Syndrome is caused by which chromosomal abnormality?
e: Patau Syndrome is Trisomy - 13.

q: Edwards Syndrome is also known as:
e: Edwards Syndrome is Trisomy - 18.

q: Klinefelter Syndrome is an example of a:
e: Klinefelter Syndrome (47, XXY) is an example of Sex chromosome Aneuploids.

q: The chromosomal makeup of a patient with Klinefelter Syndrome is typically:
e: Klinefelter Syndrome is characterized by a 47, XXY karyotype.

q: Turner Syndrome is an example of a:
e: Turner Syndrome (45, XO) lacks one sex chromosome, making it a monosomy.

q: The chromosomal makeup of a patient with Turner Syndrome is typically:
e: Turner Syndrome is characterized by a 45, XO karyotype.

q: Which of the following is NOT a type of structural chromosomal mutation?
e: Aneuploidy is a numerical mutation. Structural mutations include Deletion, Duplication, Inversion, and Translocation.

q: A structural mutation involving the loss of a chromosome segment is called a:
e: Deletion: the loss of a chromosome segment.

q: When a chromosome segment is present more than once in the same chromosome, it is called:
e: Duplication: the presence of a chromosome segment more than once in the same chromosome.

q: A structural mutation where a chromosome segment is reversed end to end is called an:
e: Inversion: a chromosome segment is reversed end to end.

q: The movement of a chromosome segment to a nonhomologous chromosome is known as:
e: Translocation: the movement of a chromosome segment to a nonhomologous chromosome.

q: Cancer is a genetic disease that arises primarily from mutations controlling:
o: Only the physical appearance of cells
e: Cancer arises from mutations in genes controlling many aspects of cellular function.

q: One of the fundamental properties shared by all cancer cells is:
o: Inability to survive outside the primary organ
e: A fundamental property of cancer cells is abnormal cell growth and division (cell proliferation).

q: The propensity of cancer cells to spread and invade other parts of the body is called:
e: Metastasis is the propensity to spread and invade other parts of the body.

q: Cell proliferation in the context of cancer refers to:
o: The spread of cells to distant organs
e: Cell proliferation refers to abnormal cell growth and division.

q: A key difference between cancer and other genetic diseases is that cancer is predominantly caused by mutations in:
e: Cancer is caused by mutations that occur predominantly in somatic cells.

q: Because cancer mutations occur predominantly in somatic cells, they are usually:
o: Only found in germ line cells
e: Since cancer mutations are predominantly somatic, they are not inherited by the next generation.

q: A condition involving a gain of two extra homologous chromosomes is best described as:
e: Tetrasomy (2n+2) involves the gain of an extra pair of homologous chromosomes.

q: Which of the following describes an Aneuploidy?
e: Aneuploidy is an abnormal number of chromosomes, not a multiple of n, such as 2n-1 or 2n+1.

q: A mutation that affects only a single base pair, such as substituting A for T, is categorized generally as a:
e: Point Mutation involves the substitution of a few bases within genes.

q: If a chromosomal mutation involves moving a segment from Chromosome 4 to Chromosome 20, it is called a:
e: Translocation is the movement of a chromosome segment to a nonhomologous chromosome.

q: Which syndrome corresponds to a Trisomy 21 karyotype?
e: Down Syndrome corresponds to Trisomy 21.

q: If a male patient has a 47, XXY karyotype, he is diagnosed with:
e: Klinefelter Syndrome is characterized by a 47, XXY karyotype.

q: A female patient presenting with a 45, XO karyotype has:
e: Turner Syndrome is characterized by a 45, XO karyotype.

q: Which of the following is considered an Autosomal Aneuploid?
e: Edwards Syndrome (Trisomy 18) is an autosomal aneuploid, while Turner and Klinefelter involve sex chromosomes.
